svg.text: “What is the distinction, the disparity, the difference
between a learned disciple of the Noble Ones and an uninstructed ordinary person?”
ko viseso ko adhippayāso kiṁ nānākaraṇaṁ sutavato ariyasāvakassa assutavatā puthujjanenā
weeps beating their breast · experiences delusion
LEARNED DISCIPLE OF THE NOBLE ONES
does not grieve · does not grow weary · does not lament
does not weep beating their breast · does not experience delusion
na socati · na kilamati · na paridevati
na urattāḷiṁ kandati · na sammohaṁ āpajjati
understanding feelings as they truly are
pleasant feeling → experiences entangled with it
painful feeling → experiences entangled with it
neutral feeling → experiences entangled with it
sorrow, lamentation, pain, displeasure, and despair
pleasant feeling → experiences disentangled from it
painful feeling → experiences disentangled from it
neutral feeling → experiences disentangled from it
sorrow, lamentation, pain, displeasure, and despair
A wise and learned person is not affected by feelings of pleasure and pain;
na vedanaṁ vedayati sapañño · sukhampi dukkhampi bahussutopi
This is the great distinction in skill, between the wise and the ordinary.
ayañca dhīrassa puthujjanena · mahā viseso kusalassa hoti.
For one who has realized the truth, who is learned, who sees clearly this world and the next;
pleasing things do not stir their mind, nor does the unpleasant cause repulsion.
iṭṭhassa dhammā na mathenti cittaṁ · aniṭṭhato no paṭighātameti
They have dispelled alignment and opposition, these are gone and no longer present;
tassānurodhā athavā virodhā · vidhūpitā atthagatā na santi
having understood the unblemished, sorrowless state, the one who has crossed beyond existence comprehends rightly.
padañca ñatvā virajaṁ asokaṁ · sammā pajānāti bhavassa pāragū
